name: Kunsan Air Base
tagline: Home of the 8th Fighter Wing 'Wolf Pack' on Korea's west coast, a remote, mostly unaccompanied one-year tour.
_note: True OCONUS (South Korea), but DIFFERENT from accompanied tours: Kunsan is a REMOTE, predominantly UNACCOMPANIED 1-year tour. No command-sponsored family housing/OHA in the usual sense and no DoDEA school on base; members live in on-base dorms. Template adapted to reflect this honestly (no rent_buy, no family housing/OHA/DoDEA sections framed as normal). 8th Fighter Wing 'Wolf Pack' (F-16). Medical: 8th Medical Group clinic. Facts verified from official sources (kunsan.af.mil, 8 MDG/TRICARE, DeCA/AAFES, Wikipedia for corroborated headline facts). Medium-confidence flags: base coords (approx).

sections.title: Mission & Identity
data: Kunsan Air Base is home to the 8th Fighter Wing, the legendary 'Wolf Pack,' flying F-16s on the west coast of South Korea. It's a frontline, mission-focused base with a strong squadron culture.

Unlike most overseas assignments, Kunsan is a remote tour that is predominantly unaccompanied, typically one year, with members living in on-base dormitories. That shapes the whole experience: it's about the mission and the squadron rather than setting up a household with family.

sections.title: A Remote, Unaccompanied Tour: What to Expect

data.subtitle: Kunsan is different from a typical family OCONUS move. It's a remote, mostly unaccompanied one-year tour, so families usually do not relocate here, and there's no family housing, OHA, or on-base DoDEA school in the usual sense.
intro: Most members come on a one-year unaccompanied tour and live in on-base dormitories. Because it's a remote tour, command-sponsored dependents generally don't accompany you, so the planning is very different from an accompanied PCS.

The flip side is a famously tight squadron community (the 'Wolf Pack' culture) and strong camaraderie. If you have a family, the bigger task is planning for the separation and a possible follow-on assignment.

recommendations.advice: Members live in on-base dormitories; there isn't the usual menu of on-base family housing or economy rentals with OHA.
Since it's unaccompanied, sort out where your family will live during the tour and your finances and communication plan early.
The squadron culture and base community are a big part of what makes Kunsan; get involved.
Gunsan and the wider country are accessible on time off; trips to Seoul and beyond are part of the tour.

sections.title: Gates & Access
data: Access follows U.S. and host-nation (SOFA) rules. Visitors without a DoD ID need to be sponsored and registered through the Visitor Control Center.
Requirements for overseas installations can be stricter and change with force-protection levels, so confirm the current details on the official Kunsan Air Base site before you go.

sections.title: Housing (On-Base Dormitories)
data: Because Kunsan is a remote, predominantly unaccompanied tour, members live in on-base dormitories rather than family housing. There isn't the usual on-base family housing or economy-rental-with-OHA setup found at accompanied overseas bases.
Your unit and the dorm management office handle your room assignment when you arrive; confirm the current arrangements through your sponsor and the base.

sections.title: Schools
data: Because Kunsan is a remote, unaccompanied tour, dependents generally do not accompany members, and there is no on-base DoDEA school of the kind found at family (accompanied) overseas bases.
If you have school-age children, they typically remain at your designated location during the tour; coordinate schooling there and confirm your specific situation with your command.

data.Care: Outpatient primary and limited specialty care on base for the assigned population; inpatient and complex care via larger military hospitals in Korea and host-nation facilities

intro: A remote, unaccompanied tour has its own checklist. The focus is on-base in-processing and squaring away your family's situation back home, not setting up a household here.

steps.title: Connect with your sponsor
steps.text: Your sponsor and unit help you settle into the dorms and learn the base. Reach out before you travel.

steps.title: In-process and update DEERS
steps.text: Report to your unit and finance, update DEERS, and get overseas ID and logistics cards as required.

steps.title: Get your dorm room set up
steps.text: Coordinate your dormitory room with your unit and the dorm office; this is your home for the tour.

steps.title: Square away the home front
steps.text: Confirm where your family lives during the tour, sort finances, allotments, powers of attorney, and a communication plan for the year apart.

steps.title: Handle SOFA and getting around
steps.text: Complete SOFA documentation and learn local transportation options; many rely on base shuttles and Korea's transit rather than a personal car.

steps.title: Set up healthcare
steps.text: Enroll in the TRICARE Overseas Program so you can be seen at the 8th Medical Group clinic, with larger Korea military hospitals for specialty care.

data.q: Can my family come with me to Kunsan?
data.a: Generally no. Kunsan is a remote tour that is predominantly unaccompanied, typically one year, so command-sponsored dependents usually don't relocate here, and there's no family housing or on-base DoDEA school in the usual sense.
In on-base dormitories. The unit and dorm office handle your room assignment when you arrive.
It's the nickname of the 8th Fighter Wing at Kunsan, known for its strong squadron culture and F-16 mission.

data.q: Do I get BAH or OHA here?
data.a: Kunsan is unaccompanied, so the usual accompanied-tour housing allowances don't apply the same way. Confirm your specific entitlements (and any continued BAH for a family back home) with finance.
